### Final Translation Rinsing the mouth with clear water and drinking up to a dou without losing composure, or so it is said, the toxicity of alcohol enters through the teeth. People in the south have a preference for drinking tea. Sun Hao offered tea to Wei Zhao as a substitute for wine, and Xie An visited Lu Na and only set out tea and fruits. Northerners initially did not know about tea. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, at the Lingyin Temple on Mount Tai, there was a monk who specialized in exorcism and taught Zen practitioners to stay awake, leading many to drink tea, and thus it became a custom. A man from Chu named Lu Hongjian wrote a treatise on tea, including methods for brewing and roasting tea, and created twenty-four tea utensils, storing them in a unified container. A person named Chang Boxiong expanded on Lu Hongjian's methods. Chang Boxiong drank too much tea and thus suffered from wind disease. Some say that northerners who did not drink tea often suffered from jaundice, and later, those who drank tea often suffered from back pain and died prematurely. Emperor Wu Zetian built the Hall of Brightness in the Eastern Capital, which was 300 feet tall. Nine cauldrons representing the nine provinces were placed under the Hall of Brightness, with the central cauldron representing the province of Yu, 18 feet tall and able to hold 1,800 shi. The others followed the directions, each 14 feet tall and able to hold 1,200 shi. A total of 567,112 catties of bronze were used. Yu the Great controlled the floods and observed the Yellow River, where he saw a white-faced, long-bodied fish-like being who said, "I am the spirit of the river," and gave Yu the River Map before returning to the depths. It also said that Yao improved the Yellow and Luo rivers, and five elders spoke to each other, saying, "The River Map will come to inform the emperor of the timing. Those who know me have double pupils." The five elders then transformed into meteors and ascended to the constellation of the Pleiades. In Shandong, during the first month, they would take five girls from different families, around ten years old, and have them sleep together on one bed, covering them with a quilt and using a winnowing basket to fan them. After a long time, they would fall into a dreamlike state, some wanting to embroider, write, or manage brushes and cords. Soon they would wake up, and it was called "fanning the sky" and was used to seek skill.

Mount Heng is the Southern Peak among the Five Great Mountains. Emperor Huangdi considered Mount Qianhao a secondary peak. *Er Ya* states that Mount Qianhao is the Southern Peak. Emperor Wu of Han, due to the distance of Mount Heng, relocated the sacrificial site to Qianshan in Luyang. Some say that Mount Heng is the same as Mount Qianhao. According to *Shan Hai Jing*, Mount Heng is also known as Mount Guimao. The term "Changshan" refers to the end of the yin and the beginning of the yang, and its path is long and enduring. Hence, the northern mountain is called Changshan, also known as Heng, which produces five grains, four types of crops, and five harvests. One measure of millet soaked in pure bitter wine for three days, then steamed and dried a hundred times, can sustain a traveler for ten days with a single meal. A heavy meal can sustain a person for 490 days without hunger. Another method involves soaking one measure of rice with three jin of red stone fat in water for two to three days, then pounding it into pills the size of a jujube. These pills can be used to abstain from food. The *Lingbao Wufu Jing* uses white fresh rice, which is steamed and dried nine times. Gengsang is also known as Yousang in *Shiji*. Xia Ji, also known as Xia Ge in *Liezi*, was a minister during the reign of King Tang. *Yueling* states that consuming a certain herb makes one light and long-lived, and that the order of drinking should start with the youngest. Firecrackers originated from the burning of grass in court ceremonies. On the first full moon of the year, a ritual is performed at the door. Willow branches are inserted into the door, and food and drink, along with porridge, are offered. Chopsticks are also used in the offering. The wine cup vine comes from the Western Regions. The vine is as thick as an arm, with leaves similar to those of kudzu and flowers like those of a paulownia tree. The flowers are hard and can be used to make wine. The fruit is fragrant and beautiful, and can be used to sober one up. When one comes to the vine, they pick the flowers and make wine, and can also use the fruit to sober up. The people of the Western Regions treasure this vine, and it was brought to China by Zhang Qian during his mission to Dayuan. This is recorded in *Zhang Qian's Account of the Western Regions*.

When chrysanthemums bloom, their stems and leaves are harvested and mixed with millet to brew wine. By the ninth day of the ninth month of the following year, the wine becomes ripe and is called chrysanthemum wine. Meng Shen said that grapes, regardless of the land, when made into wine, all result in fine wine. In ancient times, someone found a bronze drum under the city of Wang Dun. Inside the drum was cast an object that looked like a ram's head, with its body inscribed with seal script. It was identified as the Fēi Lián, a mythical creature. Ancient texts were thus lost, or so it is said, large seal script originated from Shi Zhou, a historian of King Xuan of Zhou, and small seal script originated from Li Si and Hu Mu Jing during the Qin Dynasty. The Qin Dynasty used clerical script to burn books, and ancient texts were lost. Clerical script originated in the Qin Dynasty, simplifying seal script into clerical script to facilitate the writing of urgent documents. Cheng Miao in Yunyang made additions and deletions, or so it is said. A tomb was found in Linzi with a copper coffin, and on the front and outside of the coffin were inscribed characters in ancient clerical script, referring to the tomb of Duke Huan of Qi, a descendant of the Duke of Qi. Only three characters were in ancient clerical script, indicating that clerical script has existed since ancient times, not just starting in the Qin Dynasty. Pengcheng was the ancient country of Peng Zu. The *Shibenz* records that the third son of Lu Zhong was named Jian, known as Peng Zu. It is said that Peng Zu lived for eight hundred years, his life spanning many generations, and his tomb also indicates that he was a transformation of the ultimate. In the western part of Puzhen, where Emperor Shun once reigned, there was a man named Liu Dui who drew water from the river and fermented it into fragrant wine. He drank on the day when the branches were dry and pressed the wine on the day when the mulberry leaves fell. The wine thus got its name, and it was the best tribute of the region. It is often said that "Solan" is the name, and thinking of the same things as the companions, "Solan" spoken backward is "Sangluo."

In the sea, there is a mountain called Duoshuo Mountain, upon which grows a peach tree that twists and turns over a distance of three thousand li. Its branches extend northeast to the Ghost Gate, through which ten thousand ghosts enter and exit. Tú and Yú Lěi reside at the gate, holding reed ropes to feed the ghosts. Thus, on the night of the twelfth month of the year, when the New Year's Eve arrives, Tú and Yú Lěi are hung along with reed ropes on the door. Wang Chong said that Fángjī was originally the name of a mountain, where Emperor Yu toured and held a meeting. Thus, the name was given to the prefecture. Some say this is not one of the Five Great Mountains, and during the time of Yu, the people of Wu were unclothed, had their hair cut short, and had tattoos, so there were no meetings held there. Dong Zhongshu, in his work "Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn Annals," says that in the case of spring drought, a blue dragon is made on the days of Jia and Yi, eighteen chi long, placed in the center with seven small dragons, each four chi long, all facing east in the east, with a distance of eight chi between them. Eight children fast for three days, dressed in green clothes and dancing below. The lower three times follow this pattern. All burn roosters and pig tails at the north gate of the village and in the market to pray. The method for summoning rain using lizards involves filling a large jar with soil and making a wooden lizard. Children dressed in green bamboo clothes dance, singing, "Lizard, lizard, rise clouds and spew mist. If the rain pours down, let you return." King An Yang of Jiaozhou had a divine person named Gao Tong who made a bow for him. One shot killed three hundred people. The current standard weight of 92 jin for a "dan" (stone) is used as a reference. The Han balance weighs 341 jin. The current crossbow is nine dan, equivalent to ancient twenty-five dan, compared to the strength of two Wei soldiers. Pulling a bow of three dan is equivalent to ancient thirty-four jun, compared to Yan Gao's bow, which is equivalent to the strength of more than five people.

Continuation of the *Wu Bu Zhi* Volume Five *Continuation of the *Wu Bu Zhi* (Miscellaneous Notes)* Imperially Approved Collection of the Four Treasuries Continuation of the *Wu Bu Zhi* Volume Six By Li Shi of the Song Dynasty The clan of Shao Dian gave birth to the Yellow Emperor, the Yellow Emperor gave birth to Yuanxiao, Yuanxiao gave birth to Jiao Ji, Jiao Ji gave birth to Gaoxin, who is known as Emperor Ku, and Emperor Ku gave birth to Fangxun, who is known as Emperor Yao. The Yellow Emperor also gave birth to Changyi, who gave birth to Gao Yang, known as Emperor Zuan. Changyi was the younger brother of Yuanxiao and the second son of the Yellow Emperor. Emperor Zuan gave birth to Qiongchan, who gave birth to Jingkang, who gave birth to Goumang, who gave birth to Jiaoniu, who gave birth to Gushu, who gave birth to Zhonghua, known as Emperor Shun, and his brother Xiang. Emperor Zuan also gave birth to Gun, who gave birth to Wenming, known as Yu. According to the *Tang Liudian*, every three years, a sacrifice is held to Emperor Ku at Dunchou, to Emperor Yao at Pingyang, with Ji and Xi honored alongside. To Emperor Shun at Hedong, Gou Yao is honored alongside. To Emperor Yu at Anyi, Bo Yi is honored alongside. To Emperor Tang at Yanshi, Yi Yin is honored alongside. To King Wen of Zhou at Feng, Taigong is honored alongside. To King Wu of Zhou at Hao, Zhou Gong is honored alongside. To Emperor Gaozu of Han at Changling, Xiao He is honored alongside. All these sacrifices are held in the second month of spring. Every year during the spring and autumn festivals, the County Magistrate of Qingcheng Mountain in Shuzhou offers vegetarian sacrifices and leads the procession. According to the *Qin Records*, during the reign of Fu Jian, a villager in Gaolu County dug a well and found a turtle with inscriptions on its back and ancient trigrams. Fu Jian built a stone pool to keep it in, and after sixteen years, it died. He took its bones to ask about auspiciousness and misfortune, naming it the guest turtle. Once in a dream, it said, "I will return to Jiangnan," but did not meet my fate and died in Qin. The turtle lives for thirty-six thousand years before ending, and this is a sign of the fall of Former Qin.

A turtle native to the mountains, known as the Qin turtle, does not enter the water. In contrast, a divine turtle found in the water is one foot long, and both are considered fine turtles. When this turtle sees a snake, it sucks and eats it. Li De said: Yang Xiong's *Records of the Kings of Shu* mentions that Zhang Yi built a city following the path of a turtle. Upon arriving, he learned that the turtle shell was hidden in a wine cellar. Yuwen Yu said: I once served as a minor official in charge of the wine cellar and saw the turtle shell, which was six feet long. In the early Yuanhe period, Gao Chongwen cut it to make a belt and breechclout. In the southeastern part of Yewang County, there is a Confucius Temple with a stele. In the first year of the Taihe era during the Wei dynasty, Kong Lingdu, seeing that the old structure had been destroyed, sought to restore it. The magistrate of Yewang, Fan Zhong, loved the temple and reported this to the governor of Hedong, Yuan Zhen, and the governor, Gao Yun. They proposed to erect a stele in the temple. The secretary, Liu Ming, and the deputy governor, Lu Cizhen, along with the clerk, Xiang Ban, and the diviner, Gou Linggui, decided that since Confucius, the Great Sage, was too great to be honored by a stele inscription, it should be recorded. Confucius, upon hearing about the killing of Mingdu, turned back and returned. The Jin dynasty thought about it and erected a temple stele, saying that the Kong family, officials in Luoyang, remembered their sage ancestor and built a temple, just as Liu Lei moved to Lu and built a Yao temple on the mountain. Raptors are small males and large females, while common birds are large males and small females. The kingfisher can control water, so it stays near water and does not harm other creatures. The dianthus can perform witchcraft to ban snakes. The woodpecker encounters a worm and draws a symbol with its beak, causing the worm to emerge. The magpie has a nest in a tree, and raptors cannot see it. The swallow carries soil to avoid the days of Wu and Ji, making its nest sturdy and not prone to falling. The crane has a long water stone, allowing it to raise fish in its nest without the water drying up. The swallow dislikes the magpie and carries it to place it in its nest. The egret has long feathers, and people in Jiangdong use them to make fans, calling them "white heron fans." The tiger eagle can fly and capture tigers and leopards, with a body as large as a cow and wings spanning two zhang.

The Shè Cǎo, also known as Lù Cōng, has flowers called Yí Nán. According to the *Fēng Tǔ Jì* (Record of Local Customs), pregnant women who wear its flowers will give birth to boys. Li Wèi Gong said that the golden money flower harms the eyes. The peony was initially not recorded in texts but only mentioned in herbal medicine. After Empress Wu Zetian of the Tang Dynasty, the Luoyang peonies began to flourish. Poets Shen Quanqi, Song Zhiwen, Yuan Zhen, and Bai Juyi also did not mention them. Liu Yuxi wrote a poem about the peonies in Yu Chao'en's residence, but only said a cluster of thousands. Xie Lingyun said there were many peonies in the bamboo groves of Yongjia. Now the peonies in Yue are far inferior to those in Luoyang. Some say what Xie Lingyun called peonies are now known as peony, especially abundant in Wu and Yue. The plants called Qǐ Jú, Shān Yù, and Niú Bàng are considered fine vegetables by Daoists. The lilies in Jianghuai have large roots and sweet taste, while in the south, they are slightly bitter. Next, cassia seeds, happy trees, and locust buds can also be eaten. Sun Simiao considers the happy tree as the daylily. Ji Shuyue considers the happy tree and the daylily as two plants that can alleviate anger and forget worries. The Shān Xiāo, also known as Shān Sāo, is called *Shén Yì Jīng* (Book of Strange Things) as **, and the *Yǒng Jiā Jùn Jì* (Yongjia County Records) call it a ghost. It is also called Mountain Camel, Dragon, Washed Meat,  Meat, Glow, Flying Dragon, and Blue Bird, also known as the Bird of Governance. Its nest is as large as a five-dou container, decorated with red and white earth, resembling a target. Those who offend it will be served by tigers, harmed, and their houses burned. In the past, during a flood, they ate the bark of the capital's trees, starved to death, and transformed into birds, the descendants of the surnames Yao, Wang, and Wang. It is taboo to go up onto the roof in May. It is said that in May, people shed their skin. Seeing one's shadow on the roof indicates that the soul will leave. In the two Guang regions, there is a plant called Hú Màn. If swallowed with fast water, it will cause immediate death; if swallowed with slow water, it will cause gradual death. To kill a venomous snake, cover it with this grass and sprinkle water on it. Fungi will grow on it, and the powder from these fungi will become a deadly poison.

A Northern man took advantage of a Southern woman and, upon her request to return, placed poison in her food with the agreement that she would return after a fixed year and month. If she did not return, the poison would be released, causing her death. This was called the "fixed-year drug," and Southern travelers should be aware of it. During the Song dynasty, Liu Liang attempted to create an immortal elixir that required white toads and white bats. Upon obtaining and consuming it, he died immediately. In the Tang dynasty, Chen Zhen obtained a bat as large as a crow, ate it, and died that very night from a severe illness. A small spider with long legs, known as "Xiao Shao" or "Chang Fang," is commonly called "Xizi." Lu Ji, in his poetry, referred to it as "Chang Jiao," and people in Jingzhou and Henan called it "Ximu." Duan Chengshi said that a charm for the night god could ward off bad dreams. The incantation is "Po Shan Po Yan Di Qing Tai," with a small name of twenty-three, indicating a birth date of the twenty-third day of the first month. This day is celebrated as the "Thousand Autumns Festival." Officials would mention "two ten-day periods and three days" or "twenty-two" when referring to this date, but would not directly address the emperor. According to the "Biography of the Virtuous," there was a respectful man from Lu named Fan, who was seventy years old. His respectfulness increased with age; in winter, he walked in the shade, and in summer, he walked in the sun. During one meal, he would get up three times. During the reign of Duke Mu of Qin, someone dug up an object resembling a sheep and intended to offer it. On the way, he encountered two children who said it was called "Yan," a creature that lived in the ground and ate dead people's brains. To kill it, one should use the southeast branch of a cypress tree to strike its head. This is why cypress trees are planted in tombs, and it is also said that cypress is the "court of ghosts." During the reign of Emperor He of Han, the South Sea presented lychees and longans. They were transported with a station every ten miles and a relay every five miles. The "Records of Jiaozhou" say that longans are five to six zhang tall, similar to lychees but smaller. Xie Cheng wrote that Tang Qiang, styled Boyou, submitted a memorial advising against the offering of lychees, longans, and fresh produce, as the rapid transportation by relay horses caused deaths. The two fruits were brought to the palace, but it was unlikely they would prolong life or increase longevity. The emperor agreed.

In the early period of Wei, ancient texts were transmitted from Shan Dan Chun's stele, but the original method of Shan Dan Chun was lost. The stele was eight feet long and four feet wide, and there were forty-eight stele stones, each thirty feet wide. Emperor Wen of Wei added six inscribed stones to the collection, and Lu Ji inscribed a separate stone in the lecture hall, which included the names of Cai Yong, Han Shuo, Gao Tang Xi, and other disciples of the Imperial Academy. Another stone was placed in the outer gate, and yet another stone was erected in the eighth year of Yang Jia, stating that the Imperial Academy was built in the twenty-seventh year of Jian Wu. In the sixth year of Yong Jian, the emperor issued an edict to repair the Imperial Academy, employing 112,000 workers, and the work was completed in the eighth month of the ninth year of Yang Jia. In the second year of Tai Shi, a stone was erected detailing the establishment of the Imperial Academy. Confucius said, "The movement of the Dry (Qian) is direct and still, the movement of the Earth (Kun) is open and still. The root of heaven is the daily entry and exit of the sea through the tailgate." It is said that Fei Changfang obtained a talisman from Hu Gong, which he used to subdue a hundred ghosts. Later, a ghost stole the talisman and used it to kill Fei Changfang. A Taoist priest who undergoes "corpse liberation" is said to have passed through the moon. Li Bi, while living on Mount Heng, served Master Ming Zan of the Northern School. Master Zan said that those who wish to learn should first break their writing utensils. Master Zan was known for his laziness, and the monks made him watch the salt rain flow into the pool. The monks drove him away, but he did not get angry. In winter, he would lie in front of the stove and not get up, and the monks would pour porridge on his head, which he would eat. He was called "Lazy Zan" and wrote the *Mingxin Lun*. During the Eastern Han, Cui Zhu wrote the *Yi Lin* (Book of Changes), consisting of sixty-four chapters. Cui Zhu was the ancestor of Cui Yin. It is also known as the *Gu Lin* or *Xiang Lin*. A white horse is still just a black cloth; it is all about name and reality. *Continued Catalogue of Rare and Strange Things, Volume Six*

The Four Treasuries Volume Seven of the Continued Book of Marvels By Li Shi of the Song dynasty The chapter on the "valley spirit" (谷神) being immortal and used without effort is entirely attributed to the Yellow Emperor. This chapter is found in the "Tao Te Ching" (五千文), but it may not be exclusively written by Laozi. In the first month, do not eat raw green onions; in the third month, do not eat small garlic; in the fourth month, do not eat large garlic; in the fifth month, do not eat leeks; in the sixth and seventh months, do not eat cinnamon, as it can cause bloody diarrhea; in the eighth and ninth months, do not eat ginger along with liver, heart, and lungs; in the tenth month, do not eat pepper; in the eleventh and twelfth months, do not eat armored plants, which include the spleen and kidneys. The eyes are the mirror of the body, and the ears are the windows of the body. If one looks too much, the mirror becomes dim; if one listens to too many things, the windows close. The face is the courtyard of the spirit, and the hair is the brilliance of the brain. When the heart is sad, the face becomes dry; when the brain diminishes, the hair turns white. Vital energy is the spirit of the body, and wisdom is the treasure of the person. When one labors too much, the vital energy disperses; when one is preoccupied with worldly affairs, wisdom fades. When a person dies, their form must be observed as if they were still alive: the feet do not turn blue, the skin does not wrinkle, the eyes do not lose their luster, and the hair falls out completely, leaving only the bones, which undergo a process called "尸解" (shijie). In the third year of Wu De, a man from Jin Prefecture named Ji Shanxing encountered a white-clad old man on Yangjiao Mountain. The old man called out to Ji Shanxing and said, "Tell the Tang Emperor on my behalf: I am Laozi, your ancestor." Emperor Gaozu then built a temple in honor of him. Emperor Gaozong later posthumously honored him as the Emperor Xuan Yuan. Emperor Ming Huang personally annotated the "Tao Te Ching" and ordered scholars to study it. Both the eastern and western capitals, as well as various prefectures, established temples dedicated to Emperor Xuan Yuan. The capital's temple was called the Xuan Yuan Palace, while the temples in the prefectures were called the Purple Extreme Palace. Later, the western capital's temple was renamed the Great Clarity Palace, and the eastern capital's temple was renamed the Great Micro Palace, both of which housed students. Lettuce (莴菜) grows in the land of Xie and is poisonous; no insects dare to approach it. If a snake passes over it and accidentally touches it, its eyes will become blind, and it will be unable to see. If someone is poisoned by it, only ginger juice can counteract the poison. If one eats yellow catfish and then drinks chrysanthemum herb (荆芥) soup, they will die.

Plants that move grow but do not flourish; why do people who guide and move their bodies live long and not die? The rulers Yao, like preserved meat, and Shun, like dried meat, were tall, while the tyrants Jie and Zhou were short. Wang Chong said: If there is a god of thunder and an instrument for thunder, how can it be called a god? In January, yang energy begins to move, so thunder starts; in May, yang energy is at its peak, so thunder is loud; in autumn and winter, yang energy declines, so thunder is hidden. When yang energy is active, yin energy rises against it, and the two forces clash, creating a toxic force that can kill people or break trees. People can die from being struck by lightning; try pouring a bucket of water on a fire being smelted; the intense fire will produce a thunderous sound. Some say that when lightning strikes metal or stone, they are burned and melted, but why does the lacquer vessel not break? Taoists say that when lightning strikes a stone, they throw it into a well; the stone burns, and the cold water causes a loud sound. Yan Hui and Confucius both ascended Mount Tai and looked southeast at the Wu Gate. Confucius saw a white horse and pointed to it, asking Yan Hui, "Do you see the Wu Gate?" Yan Hui said, "I see it, but it has the appearance of a tethered rope." Confucius touched his eyes and stopped him. Yan Hui's hair turned white and his teeth fell out, and he died of illness. This was because his energy was not up to that of a sage, and he was overexerted. The plant *jia* resembles *zhulian* but is smaller and red. The plant *sha* resembles a pear but is sour and astringent. Deer eat the leaves of *jiangzhu* and *yujue*, and their flesh naturally takes on the aroma of pepper and orange. In Jiangdong, *lai* is called a vine, resembling kudzu but coarser. The tree *pien* resembles *yuzhang*, and *zhu* is a type of oak. Some say it is a type of *zao*. The fruit of *yan* resembles a crabapple, and *you* resembles an orange but is sour. The *Huainanzi* says: The fierce resemble knowledge but are not truly knowledgeable; the foolish resemble gentlemen but are not truly gentlemen; the rash resemble courage but are not truly courageous. People differ from each other like jade and stone, or like the *kuai* and *xian* plants. Thus, it is easy to judge people. Those who confuse people are like *xiang* and *gao* roots, or like *shechuang* and *mihua*.

The Mingjia, also known as the Lijia, was used by sage kings to determine the number of days and months. The red grass, shaped like a small mulberry tree, grows three or four feet tall with branches and leaves all red, the juice like blood. It grows and falls monthly like the Mingjia, repeating in cycles. It can be used to dye red and create patterns of black and white. Gold and silver, when dipped in its juice, can be made into round lumps; when thrown into water, they become golden liquid; when added to wine, they turn into jade-like liquor, which, when drunk, grants immortality, as described in the *Baopu Zi*. The people of Biao Kingdom do not cultivate the seeds of the Sera tree; when broken open, they contain fine fibers like willow fluff, which are woven into fabric called Sera Longduan. The barbarian method of cooking salt into lumps, each lump counted as a unit. Tea comes from the silver-producing mountains, harvested at any time, mixed with pepper and ginger, and brewed for drinking. Native gold is found in the mountains near Chang, harvested in spring or winter by digging pits in the mountain, filled with water and silt in summer, and then sifted in autumn. Large pieces weigh one or two catties, smaller ones at least three or four taels. Eight-tenths go to the government, the rest to the private sector, with accumulated labor exempt from taxes. Flourish gold is found in Lishui River and Heishan Creek; those with crimes are sent to the gold washing place for heavy labor. Silver is mined in the mountains of Fangtong River, with private smelting offices taking three-tenths. Tin is found in the mountains of Nuo River. Su Dan, a native of Chencounty, once herded cows with other children, taking turns to be the leader. Whenever it was his turn, the cows would return on their own. Later, he left his mother to become an immortal. He also said that during an epidemic, digging a well and drinking from it could prevent illness. The Taoist Tan Zixiao has the skill of observing lights, predicting people's fortune and misfortune, always accurately.

Guo Ban's *Shi Yu* records that during the Huangchu era of Wei, robbers dug up the tomb of Wu Rui and saw his body, whose appearance and clothing were all like those of a living person. Wu Ping, a grave robber from Shouchun, saw Nanman Xiaowei Wu Gang and said, "Your appearance is like that of King Wu Rui of Changsha, but you are slightly shorter." Wu Gang was startled and said, "He is my ancestor." From the time of Wu Rui's death to the time the tomb was dug up was four hundred years, and then another forty years had passed until Wu Gang. The Dan River has a stone cave where large bats often hang upside down. Those that are a hundred years old, if hung upside down and eaten, can make a person become a god. Tao Mengju said that those who study the Dao should live in the mountains and raise white dogs and white chickens, which can ward off evil. The Su Fang wood naturally produces purple powder from insect excrement. In the twenty-first year of Qin, twelve golden statues were cast. The origin of Buddhism is based on Huo Qubing's campaign against Xutu Wang, during which he captured a golden statue used for worshipping heaven. Emperor Wu of Han considered it a god and placed it in Ganquan Palace. In the first year of Huangchu in Wei, the bronze statues in Chang'an were too heavy to move. Some said that the bronze statues wept, so they were left in the south of Bachi. Someone saw Jizixun and the elders together rubbing the bronze statues and said, "Just saw the casting at that time, calculated from that day, it has been nearly five hundred years." Dong Zhuo destroyed nine of them into coins, and Fu Jian destroyed two of them into coins. One of them, the people pushed and placed it in the river north of Shanxi. Some say that the topknots of the statues often appeared, but when the Jin army arrived, the topknots no longer appeared. Chen Zhengmin chose Chen Tuan, Li Du, Lin Bu, and Wei Ye, all reclusive scholars. The mystic and ethereal Master Chen Tuan practiced breath control and abstained from food for over twenty years. Sometimes he slept for over a hundred days without waking. During the Taiping Xingguo period, he was summoned into the court and then sent back to the mountains. One day, he wrote a memorial saying, "I, Chen Tuan, the great cycle is coming to an end, the holy court is difficult to stay in. On the twenty-second day of this month, I will transform into a god on Lianhua Peak." He died as scheduled. Seven days after his death, his limbs were still warm. Five-colored clouds covered the cave entrance for a month without dispersing. He was already over a hundred years old.

The Transmission of Immortals by Liu Xiang, the Record of Immortals by Ge Hong, the Continuation of Immortals by Shen Bin, and the Collection of Immortals by Zeng Cao compile stories of immortals and legendary figures from different dynasties that are recorded in the current dynasty. Among those favored by the ancestors of the current dynasty are Zhu Dun, Lü Yan, Chen Tuan, He Yuan, and others. Famous officials such as Ouyang Xiu, Liu Fan, and Shi Yanian are also included. He Yuan served as an external officer of the Water Department during the Later Jin dynasty. During the early Tian Sheng period, he sent his disciple Yu Cheng to the imperial court to present statues of Buddha and Laozi, valued at tens of thousands of coins. There is a woman with fur on her body, known as the "Fur Woman," who has been seen by hunters and their teachers on Mount Hua generation after generation. She claims to have been a palace woman during the Qin Dynasty. Chen Tuan, also on Mount Hua, was sometimes criticized for associating with the Fur Woman. Kong Anguo compiled a list of seventy-two disciples of Confucius, Liu Xiang compiled a list of seventy-two immortals, Huangfu Shian compiled a list of seventy-two recluses, and Chen Changwen compiled a list of seventy-two elderly scholars. Li Bi abstained from food for five or six years and could walk on a folding screen. He could direct his breath to extinguish a candle. When he moved, his bones made a tinkling sound, known as "lock bone." Yu Zi, also known as Xiong, was a teacher to King Wen of Zhou and met him at the age of ninety. He said, "If you ask me to hunt animals, I am too old; but if you ask me to advise on state affairs, I am still young." According to Ge Xian Gong, the *Sushu* (White Book) was passed down from He Shang Gong to Emperor Wen of Han. The *Yinfu* (Dark Symbol) was not what Zhang Zifang received from Yellow Stone Gong. Another version says *Sanlue* (Three Strategies) was written by Zhang Zifang, not given to him. Some say that since He Shang Gong taught Emperor Wen of Han, it wouldn't be found in Zhang Zifang's tomb. Yellow Stone Gong's red book describes eight kinds of people who should be punished and four types of behavior that should be avoided: those who are greedy, cruel, treacherous, and violent; those who are deceitful, cunning, and arrogant; those who betray favors and rely on themselves; those who associate with the unworthy and envy the virtuous.

### Final Translation Once upon a time, there was a man who loved the Dao but did not know how to seek it. He only worshipped and knelt to a dry tree every day and night, always saying to beg for immortality. He did this for twenty-eight years without tiring. Suddenly, the dry tree blossomed, and the flowers had sap as sweet as honey. Someone taught him to eat the flowers and sap, so he took them and ate them together. After eating, he became an immortal. Liu Shao-weng often entered Taihuashan to worship the mountain like this for twenty years. He suddenly met the Western Peak Elder and was taught the path to immortality. One person went to the river every day to worship the river water like this for ten years. The River Lord and River God then met him, gave him ten pairs of white jade, and taught him the method of walking on water without drowning. Coix seed, also known as bamboo pearl, is steamed until steam distills, dried in the sun, and then made into rice or noodles to prevent hunger. The method of making dried ginger involves soaking it in water for three days, then placing it in running water for six days, removing the skin, and then drying it in the sun before placing it in jars or bottles, which is called 'fermentation.' Epimedium, also known as fairy spleen, is caused by a goat eating it a hundred times a day. Newly harvested mugwort is made into dried vegetables, which are excellent for making dumplings. Eating three or five dumplings with rice can effectively treat all kinds of ghostly illnesses. Storax, according to the *Bencao*, is said to grow in the Central Plains. Tao Hongjing said it is lion dung, and others say it is a mixture of various fragrant juices. It is not a single substance but comes from the Western Regions, like sandalwood, heavy as stone, and is also known as mountain grapes.

People from Jiangdong are often called "杨栁" or "杨叶短栁叶长," which is a play on words or a riddle. The High Seat monk refuses to speak in Chinese. Someone asked him about the meaning, and Jianwen replied, "This is a simple way to respond, but it's too complicated for monks. One of them is named Shilim." Song Yun, the High Seat monk, is a Western Regions person. According to the *Three Kingdoms* tradition, a prince gave his position to his younger brother and became a monk. He arrived in China during the Yongjia period. During the reign of Emperor Shun, a man from Langya named Gong Chong presented his teacher Yu Ji, who had obtained a divine book of 170 volumes called *Tai Ping Qing Ling Shu* (Peaceful Governance Scripture) by the waters of Quyang. The book combines the principles of Yin-Yang and the Five Elements with shamanistic practices. Later, Zhang Jiao had a copy of this book. According to the *Jiangbiao* (Records of Jiangdong), the Daoist Yu Ji came from the East and established a study hall in Wu, where he burned incense and read books. Sun Ce ordered his execution. It was said that in the past, Zhang Jin, the Governor of Jiaozhou, often wore a red headscarf, played the qin, burned incense, and read Daoist books that were considered heretical. He was killed by local tribes, which was very detrimental. The *True Rites* (Zhen Gao) mentions that those who swallowed the flowers of the Linggan tree and built tombs in the hills were Xianmenzi, Gaohuzi, and Hongyajianshi. Xianmenzi's tomb is in Youzhou's Yuyang, Gaohuzi's tomb is in Zhongshan, and Hongyajianshi's tomb is in Wuwei. The people of these three counties all claim that these are the tombs of ancient deceased people. The tomb of Prince Ziqiao is in Jingling. During the Warring States period, when someone opened the tomb, they only found a sword. According to the *History of the Han Dynasty*, the Yangtze River and its nine tributaries have one source but three outlets. Yang Xiong wrote the *Yizhou Zhen* (Admonition to the State of Yi), which describes the rugged mountains of Minshan, an ancient region called Liangzhou. In the north of Lucheng, there was a Confucian academy, and in Shu, there was the Wen翁讲堂 (Wen翁 Lecture Hall). In the north of the Bohai Sea, there was the Zheng Sinao儒林讲堂 (Zheng Sinao Confucian Lecture Hall). The term "宅" (residence) is also used to mean "第" (rank), indicating a hierarchy. According to the *Book of Han*, Emperor Cheng had a viewing pavilion with painted halls, and the Wei dynasty had a thatched pavilion.

### Translation **Song Dynasty, Li Shi, compiled** In the Later Han dynasty, Xiang Kai presented a memorial during the Yanyi period, stating that there is a temple for Huang-Lao and Buddha in the palace. If the emperor does not abandon his desires, he will not receive his mandate. Some say Laozi entered the northwest and became a Buddha. The Buddha does not stay three nights under a mulberry tree; he does not wish to long dwell in affection. A heavenly deity sent a beautiful woman to the Buddha, who said, "This is just a leather bag filled with blood," and did not look at her. The Han emperor asked Chen Fan and Xu Zhi, who should come first. Chen Fan replied that Xu Zhi was born into a noble family and did not need to be carved out; Xu Zhi was outstanding and should come first. Fu Sheng was a scholar of the Qin dynasty. Emperor Wen of Han compiled the Five Classics and the Great Commentary on the *Shangshu*. He appointed Ouyang Sheng and others to study the *Shangshu* with Fu Sheng. There is a tomb of Fu Sheng in Lü Water. The *Jin Shu* (Book of Jin) records in the section on geography that people in Liaoxi saw a floating coffin on the Liao River and wanted to break it open. The coffin said, "I am the Lord of Guxu." They asked why they should break it open, and they built a shrine for it. Some say that during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, the governor of Liaoxi, Lián Fān, had a dream about it. Shen Bin retired from his post as a *shangshu* and lived in Gao'an, Jiangxi. He planted a tree in a flat field and warned his sons, "You must bury me here." His descendants felled the tree and dug deep, finding a stone coffin with eight characters carved on it: "Kaiceng Year Two, a coffin for a long life. One place. Buried here." Mencius disliked music, so he turned his chariot away from the town of Chaoguo. The *Lunzhuan* (Commentary on the Analects) says that the town is named Chaoguo. Confucius did not stop there, and seventy of his disciples covered their eyes. Zai Yu alone looked back, and Zilu (Subu) fell from his chariot. Song Jun said that Zilu was troubled by Zai Yu looking back at the ominous place, so he kicked him. During the Qi dynasty, someone opened an ancient tomb and found an inscription: "Twenty sons of Qingzhou, a daughter of the East Sea, and a wife of Jixi." Jia Hao of Hedong believed it was the daughter of Sima Yue, who married Jixi. This was verified as true.

In the Eastern Capital, at the bustling intersection of the Zhiye Lane, there was a tomb of Wang Rong, a nobleman from the Jin dynasty. During the Sui dynasty, a wine merchant dug a pit near the tomb and found an inscription that read, "The tomb of Wang Jun, the Duke of Anfeng, who was a Grand Tutor and Minister of Works." The tomb of King Quji of Guangchuan was opened by King Wuzi of Wei. It was very shallow and narrow, with no coffin or casket. There was a stone bed, and beneath it was entirely covered in mica. On the bed lay two bodies, one male and one female, both around twenty years old. They were lying facing east, naked, and their appearance was like that of living people. Their hair and teeth were also like those of living people. The king was so frightened that he dared not approach. In the tomb of King Ling of Zhou, the bodies were also not decayed. There was only one jade toad. The tomb of King You of Zhou was very high and majestic. The mica was deep by more than a foot, and over a hundred bodies were lying on top of each other, all uncorrupted. There was only one man among them, and the rest were women. In the tomb of Luan Shu, there was a white fox. The king struck it and injured its foot. The king later fell ill and died from his foot injury. Xiao Yingshi was traveling with his companions when they reached Guazhou. In a boat, two young men looked at each other and said, "You look very much like Prince Zhonglie of Poyang." Xiao Yingshi was the grandson of Prince Zhonglie. He immediately introduced himself to the two young men, who said, "We have known your ancestor for a long time." They quickly left, carrying their belongings. Xiao Yingshi returned north to Xuyi, where officials captured five or six tomb robbers. Xiao Yingshi recognized the two young men from the river and questioned them. They said, "We once opened the tomb of Prince Zhonglie of Poyang. At the entrance, we found a noble person whose appearance was like that of Xiao Yingshi. This is similar to the story of Wu Gang, who is like Wu Rui. The story of Wu Rui is recorded in the *Shui Jing*. Xiao Yingshi also mentioned this in his *Ji Yi Ji*." Mount Jiu Zhen is home to many large snakes, known as beard-like snakes. They are ten zhang long and have a circumference of seven or eight chi. They eat deer and antelope, and their horns and bones are used to make brocade. The people of the region kill them with large bamboo spears and eat them. According to *Yang Shi Nan Yi Wu Zhi*, the beard-like snake is both large and long, with colorful and varied patterns like brocade. It eats dogs and deer, and its skin is used to make brocade. It is served as food or drink at banquets.

In Wuchang County, there was a man who, while traveling on the road, found a small snake and named it "Dàn." As it grew, it began to bite people, and the locals captured and imprisoned it in jail. The man, however, carried the snake and fled to the county, where it transformed into a fish of the lake county official. In the second year of the reign of Emperor Guangwu of Han, Xian Yu Ji served as the governor of Qinghe. He began to construct a public office but died before completing it. After his death, the next governor, Zhao Gao, calculated the cost of labor at two million. Huang Bing Liu Shì, however, stated the cost as four million. Xian Yu Ji then appeared as a ghost, entering the government office during the day, accompanied by others. He confronted Zhao Gao and Huang Bing Liu Shì, and they recalculated the cost, which was ultimately hidden by Huang Bing Liu Shì. Xian Yu Ji then wrote a petition to justify himself, stating that he, a high-ranking official, did not uphold the principles of the common people and sat arrogantly, leaving his legacy to the world. He sought prominence through deceit, stealing the insignia of office and disgracing the heavenly official. The ghost spoke to him, and thirty miles away, his chariots and horses disappeared, and Zhao Gao and Huang Bing Liu Shì fell to the ground and died. Zhao Gao reported the incident, and the emperor ordered a replacement to investigate the hidden case. An elderly man crossed the Chao Ge River in the morning and felt cold due to the weakness of his bone marrow. He cut off his leg to examine the marrow. King Zhou had new music and decadent songs composed in the city of Chao Ge. The *Records of the Grand Historian* states that the music of Chao Ge was not played at the right time. Between the states of Yu and Guo, Fu Yue lived in the cave of Fu Yan, which was commonly known as the "Cave of the Sage." Liu Xiang narrated in the *Yanzi Chunqiu* that Gu Yezhi said, "I once crossed the river, and a turtle carried the left side into the turbulent waters of the Dishu, and I followed and killed it." Confucius sang by the Di River, saying, "The Di River spreads, the wind raises waves, and boats capsize, piling on top of each other." In the old county of Jixian, which was part of the Jiquan region, there was a stone inscription on the bank of the river, known as the "Panchi," where Jiang Ziya fished. There was also the "Taigong Spring" and the "Taigong Temple." In Muyu, the *Zhu Shu* records that there was a tomb of Bi Gan, a loyal minister of the Shang dynasty. On the tomb, there was a stone inscription in clerical script, which said, "The tomb of the minister of the Shang dynasty, Bi Gan." The inscription only recorded this, and Emperor Gaozu and Emperor Wen of Han each erected a stele in honor of him.

Yan She, courtesy name Hongdu, had an elder brother named Yan Jie who fell ill and died at the doctor's house. Those who came to mourn and lead the funeral procession stumbled and said, "My lifespan is not yet over, but I have taken too many medicines, which have injured my five organs." When Yan She returned, his wife dreamed of him and said, "I will be reborn. Quickly open the coffin and scrape the coffin with my hands, scratching my nails until they are all injured. The breath is very weak. The food and drink needed were entrusted to the dream." Yan She cared for him for thirteen years without leaving the house, and Yan Jie ultimately did not revive. In the two Guang regions, on the deep ravines and stone walls, there are ghost shadows like light ink paintings. The boatmen think these are their ancestors and worship them without being lax. During the years of Wu and Ji, swallows avoid these days, and bats hibernate during the years of Geng and Xin. If one encounters these days three times in a year, the mulberry trees will be weak, and the horses will suffer. Confucius said, "Even ten miles away from the mountain, the sound of cicadas can still be heard. Governing should avoid noise and favor order." Earthworms make a long, continuous sound underground. In the east of the river, they are called "singing girls." Loquats have a sour taste and are good for treating muscle cramps. Tao Yuanming said that if one calls out the name of the loquat or writes the character for loquat when experiencing muscle cramps, it will heal immediately. Glue made from grain can be used to bind cinnabar, and it is also described as a binding agent for metals and stones. Oranges and tangerines are grafted in the north, while pomegranates are grafted in the east. The *Lüshi Chunqiu* says, "A good learner is like King Qi of Qi who eats chicken; he must eat the feet of thousands of chickens before he can be satisfied." Murong Wei had a horse that lived for forty-nine years without losing its vigor. He cast a bronze statue of the horse and personally wrote an inscription and praise for it. In the country of Babali, there is a strange beast called "camel cow." Its skin resembles that of a leopard, and its hooves are like those of a cow. It has no hump, a neck nine feet long, and a height of over ten feet.

### Final Translation **Water Musk in the Early Tianbao Era** In the early Tianbao era, a water musk deer was captured by a gamekeeper and ordered to be kept. The musk from its navel, when dripped into a bucket of water and used to sprinkle clothes, would leave a fragrance that did not fade even after the clothes were worn out. Each time it was extracted by piercing the animal and adding real cinnabar, the scent was twice as strong as that of land musk. This differs slightly from the previous account of the water musk and is recorded here. **Zhang Hua, the Attendant-in-Ordinary** Zhang Hua, serving as an attendant-in-ordinary, had extensive knowledge of books and records. He could recall vast amounts of information, drawing maps on the ground as if they were already laid out before him. Sima Qian wished to create his own historical narrative while following the conventions of classical texts to adapt to the changing times. However, altering the ancient texts of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius) would be questionable. **Qu Yuan Drafting Laws** The King of Chu appointed Qu Yuan to draft laws, but the draft was not yet finalized. An official saw it and tried to take credit for it. Qu Yuan refused, saying, "The king appointed me to draft the laws, and everyone knows this. Every time a law is issued, I claim the credit, thinking that no one else could do it better." As a result, the king demoted Qu Yuan. **Kangcangzi** Geng Sangzi, a legendary figure, was compiled by Wang Shiyuan, who selected passages from the *Gengsang* chapter of the *Zhuangzi*. According to the preface, his ancestors found an ancient manuscript in the mountains. Tang Dynasty scholars doubted its authenticity and did not implement it. Today, the *Kangcangzi* (亢仓子) consists of three volumes. **Zhao Bin of Shu** Zhao Bin, a man from Shu, had unusual ideas about the nature of qi (气) and the meaning of *Jizi* (箕子). *Jizi* refers to the root of all things. Yan Shigu said that *Jizi* and *Ji* (箕) have the same pronunciation. **Yang Guifei's Hairstyle** Yang Guifei, the beloved concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, wore a false braid as a headdress called *yi ji* (义髻). During the reign of Emperor Xianzong, the imperial concubines tightly bound their hair, forming a style called *qiujiji* (囚髻). By the end of the Tang Dynasty, women styled their hair with two braids wrapped around their faces, known as *paojiaji* (抛家髻). **Salivation and Tears** Imagining plums can produce saliva, and eating mustard can cause tears. These are physiological responses that come from external stimuli. Longing can make one drool, and shame can make one sweat. These are physiological responses that come from internal emotions. **Luminous Fungi** The mountain of Liangchang has luminous fungi the size of beans. At night, they emit light. Eating one can illuminate the heart, and eating seven can open seven holes, allowing one to read at night. The Huayang Cave also has five types of night-bright fungi, and the Baoshan has white fungi. --- This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and readable in English, maintaining the cultural and historical context while addressing syntactic and idiomatic challenges.

Wang Rui says that since the invention of writing, there should have been a brush pen. The world believes that Meng Tian invented it, but Cui Bao says that Meng Tian made the brush pen from dried wood, deer hair, and sheep wool, which is not the same as today's rabbit hair. During the reign of Emperor Dezong, Yuan Zhen sent Xue Tao to Shu to create colorful paper, which he then used to write poems and send to Xue Tao. The paper included pine bark paper, colored sand paper, and gold powder paper, but these are no longer in use, leaving only the ten-colored silk-patterned paper. The *Xiangzhou Ji* records that south of Cai Zi's pool, there is a stone mortar used by Cai Lun in the paper-making process. Li Simu, while in Bianzhou, had a unique bone that could be used as a砚. He obtained it from a merchant from the South Seas, who said it was the spine of a centipede. Xiao Shi and Mu Gong created a type of elixir called "flying snow pill." The first transformation of this elixir was used to paint or apply, and it is now known as a type of rouge. After the Three Dynasties, purple grass was used to make rouge, and during the Zhou Dynasty, red flowers were used. Some say it originated from the阏氏. Shen Yi studied Daoism in Sichuan and traveled with his wife. According to Guan Zhong, King Wu of Zhou issued a decree for luxury, stating that only those wearing leopard skin cloaks and furs could enter the temple. As a result, leopard skins were extremely expensive, and even high-ranking officials had to pay a high price for them. Lingyang Ziming's commentary states that in spring, one eats the morning light (a metaphor for the rising sun), which is a yellow mist at sunrise. In autumn, one eats the evening light (a metaphor for the setting sun), which is a red-yellow mist after sunset. In winter, one eats the night mist (a metaphor for the night sky), which is a mist at midnight. In summer, one eats the midday sun (a metaphor for the sun at noon), which is a sun at noon in the south. Together, these are the six types of mist or light. Mozi created a wooden bird that flew for three days without landing. Gongshu Ban's cloud ladder and Mozi's flying bird are examples of their inventions.

**Translation:** Kites, when flown with a string, are said to make children open their mouths and look up, possibly to relieve some internal discomfort, though the exact meaning of "内" is unclear. During the Tongguang period, Emperor Zhuangzong sent troops to pacify Shu and seized the gold and silver of Wang Yan. He ordered all of it to be melted into gold and silver bricks, each weighing about three hundred pounds, with a hole in each brick for identification. Two people were needed to carry each brick. The craftsman who made these bricks was named Feng Gao. When Feng Gao passed through Jingnan, Gao Jixing, the ruler of Jingnan, said, "Feng Gao, these bricks belong to me. I will trick the officials into holding onto them for me." The gold and silver were stored in a single warehouse. During the Jianlong period of the Song Dynasty, the gold and silver entered the capital, and their weight and seals remained unchanged. In Chinese custom, high-quality gold is called "purple ground gold," while in barbarian custom, it is called "Yangmai gold." Stamped gold, passed down through generations as the medicine gold of King An of Huainan, often bears seal script with Liu's character. It was found in the soil of Baigong Mountain in Shouzhou, with small pieces weighing half a tael and large pieces weighing up to seven taels, each with about twenty stamps. Between Xiang and Zhao, in the former Chongling Bai Water area, golden deer hooves and horse hooves were often found. The gold hooves were hollow, with intricate carvings around the edges, shaped like dried persimmons. The locals called it "persimmon gold," and some say it was the medicine gold made by Lou Jing, which was good for mixing with medicine. Ancient jade artifacts with unique features that were not carved by human hands were often made with the Kunwu knife or toad fat. Toad fat could be obtained by grinding and cooking it to make a paste and applying it to the jade, making it soft, smooth, and easy to cut. Seven liters of winter melon seeds were placed in a silk bag and boiled three times, then dried in the sun. This process was repeated three times, and the seeds were soaked in vinegar overnight. They were then ground into powder and taken daily in a small measure, said to make one not age. A thousand-year-old celestial rhinoceros is long and sharp, with a white star at its tip. It can emit a divine aura that can penetrate the sky, allowing it to communicate with the gods and break through water, frightening chickens. According to *Baopu Zi*, there are rhinoceroses with white lines like threads that, when filled with rice, can scare chickens. If carved into fish and placed in water, the water will open three feet wide.

The People of Lebi The people of Lebi are three inches tall, have wings, and are skilled in speaking and joking, hence the country is named "Yuguo." They drink Danlu (a red dew) as a beverage, which is the dew that appears at sunrise and resembles pearls. During the reign of Emperor Wu, he practiced water warfare and constructed the Kunming Pool. While fishing, his fishing line broke and he dreamed of a fish that pulled the hook away. The next day, when he played in the pool, he saw a fish holding a rope, which he took away. The fish then returned to the pool and brought him a pair of pearls. On the mountains of Yuanqiao in the East Sea, there are ice silkworms seven inches long with scales and horns. When covered with frost and snow, they begin to spin silk, and their cocoons are multicolored. The silk they weave is fine and does not get wet when submerged in water or burn when thrown into fire. Shijueming is also known as Jiukongluo (a nine-hole snail). Eating fish roe with pork liver can be fatal. According to Chen Cangqi, the marrow in a crab's leg and the yellow substance in its shell can be used to heal broken bones and tendons when applied to the wound. In the dialect of Guilin, the term "Shou Gong" (a type of lizard) refers to a creature that can make sounds and resembles the "Hajie" (a type of gecko). Spider webs can be used to treat warts and boils, which often heal within seven days. The yuzu resembles an orange but is larger than a tangerine and has a sweet, milky taste. Long-term use of Xiong Fang (a type of herb) can lead to sudden death. Eating lotus root with yuzu can help one abstain from food. A recipe where one liang of Suhe Xiang (a type of incense) and one dou of wine are cooked together with a pear is said to harmonize the five organs. According to the Brahmins, Dong Qu (a type of plant) is similar to A Wei (a type of plant). Hu Ma (sesame) is compatible with Fu Ling (a type of fungus) and is also known as Ju Sheng (a type of herb). Ku Shen (a type of herb) is used as a tooth remedy but can cause kidney damage and make the waist feel heavy and cold. Dates and thorns both have thorns. Jing Suli (a type of rice) is the hardest among the five grains and can be easily softened with water. Cooking it into dry rice can stop diarrhea.

### Final Translation **Using Mugwort Leaves to Repel Fleas and Lice** Place mugwort leaves under your mat to repel fleas and lice. **Taoist Term for Vinegar** The immortals refer to vinegar as "Hua Chi Zuo Wei." **Removing White Hair and Preventing Black Hair Growth** White hair must be removed with tweezers and wax must be applied to prevent black hair from growing in the pores. **Medicinal Concoction** Mix equal parts of pine resin, almond, jujube flesh, and poria, and take fifty grains after meals to avoid hunger. Avoid eating pork if you have yellow gentian or platycodon. If you have poria, avoid vinegar. If you have fine ginger, avoid raw vegetables. **Ancient Mirrors** Ancient mirrors were not made purely of copper; they all contained tin. **Toxicity of Croton** Even immortals would die if they ate one seed of croton. A rat eating it for three years would weigh thirty catties. **Folk Belief About Metal** Flour gold is actually snake droppings. **Superstition About the Zheng Surname** Those with the Zheng surname should avoid certain actions on the day of the pig (亥) and use fire to ward off bad luck on that day. **Belief About Killing Snakes** On the day of the snake (巳) or in the year of the snake, one should not kill snakes. **Taoist Deities** According to Duan Chengshi, the deities Shentu and Yulu lead ten thousand spirits. **Malaria and Cultural Perceptions** The malaria spirit is small and cannot harm giants, hence the saying that heroes do not suffer from malaria. The people of Jin say that gentlemen do not suffer from malaria. The people of Shu consider intermittent malaria to be a form of servant malaria. **Classification of Pearls** According to Shen Huaiyuan's *Nan Yue Zhi*, pearls are classified into nine grades. Those measuring more than five-tenths of an inch up to eight-tenths of an inch are the highest grade. They have a lustrous appearance and one side is slightly flat, resembling a golden cover. These are called dangzhu pearls. Below dangzhu pearls are zouzhu pearls, followed by hua pearls, leiguo pearls, guanyu pearls, shui pearls, and cong pearls. **Rare Animal and Wine Making** In the second year of the Yuanfeng era (109 BCE), the Roman Empire presented a cow with spotted hooves, six feet tall, with its tail wrapped around its horns, and four ears. The insect known as the "Asa Department Wax Deer" was used to make wax. Its meat was cut and layered with stones to press out the juice, which was then mixed with herbs from Persia and other regions and fermented for several days to become wine. Drinking this wine can intoxicate one. **End of Continuation of *Zuobi Zhi* Volume Ten** --- This translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and readable in English, while preserving the cultural and historical context.

尔雅既曰释天不得不略言其趣凡有六等一曰盖天文见周髀如盖在上二曰浑天形如弹丸地在其中天包其外犹如鸡卵白之绕黄雄桓谭张衡蔡邕陆绩王肃郑之徒并所依用三曰宣夜旧说云殷代之制四曰昕天昕读为轩言天北髙南下若车之轩吴时姚信所说五曰穹天云穹隆在上虞氏所说六曰安天晋时虞喜所论郑注考灵曜云天者纯阳清明无形圣人则之制璿玑玉衡以度其象如郑此言则天是太虚本无形体但指诸星运转以为天耳 天围圜三百六十五度四分度之一案考灵曜云一度二千九百三十二里千四百六十一分里之三百四十八周天百七万一千里者是天圆周之里数也以围三径一言之则直径三十五万七千里此为二十八宿周回直径之数也然二十八宿之外上下东西各有万五千里是为四游之极谓之四表四表之内并星宿内总有三十八万七千里然则天之中央上下正半之处则一十九万三千五百里地在于中是地去天之数也
